exemplar.title: How has Twitter changed and popularized English terms over the past decade?

detailedExemplarData.title: How has Twitter changed and popularized English terms over the past decade?

subject.title: English A Lang & Lit
subject.description:
**IB English A Language & Literature** is a **Group 1** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **the study of language and literature through a wide variety of texts and media from different periods, styles, and cultures**, with a strong focus on **critical analysis of both literary and non-literary texts, examining how language choices create meaning and effect in different contexts**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paper 1** | Text analysis | 1h 15m | 2h 15m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL) |
| **Paper 2** | Comparative essay | 1h 45m | 1h 45m | 35% (SL), 25% (HL)  |
| **HL Essay** | Written assignment | N/A | 1200-1500 words | 25% (HL only) |
| **Individual Oral** | Oral presentation | 15 minutes | 15 minutes | 30% (SL), 20% (HL) |

strengths: Clear and consistent communication of the investigationâs purpose and focus throughout the essay
Research question is explicitly stated, focused, and effectively connected to each analytical section
Methodology is mostly complete and demonstrates informed selection of semantic and pragmatic analysis methods
weaknesses: Sampling criteria for tweet selection are not fully specified, limiting methodological rigor
Discussion of alternative methods (e.g., larger corpus analysis) is only briefly mentioned
Occasional digressions (extended historical overview) weaken the sustained focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates strong clarity of topic and a tightly integrated research question, meriting the upper end of the 3â4 band. Methodology is relevant and generally wellâdescribed but lacks detailed selection criteria and discussion of alternative approaches, preventing a topâband score. Overall, a score of 5 accurately reflects the workâs clear focus and mostly effective method without overstating its methodological completeness.

strengths: Sound knowledge of historical language change, Twitter affordances, and selected lexical items

weaknesses: Integration of sources is often descriptive with limited critical appraisal of authority
Reliance on nonâpeerâreviewed or popularâpress sources in places weakens scholarly depth
Conceptual explanations sometimes remain at the surface level without deeper theoretical engagement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows good mastery of content and mostly accurate use of disciplineâspecific language, placing it solidly in the 3â4 band. However, the descriptive nature of source use and limited critical evaluation prevent elevating the work into the top band. A score of 4 reflects this balance of competent knowledge and areas for deeper sourceâbased analysis.

strengths: Coherent organisation around four case studies with logical progression
Use of a reasonable variety of relevant sources to support the investigation
Effective semanticâpragmatic analyses (e.g., emoji connotation, early hashtag reception) demonstrating insightful interpretation
weaknesses: Argumentation occasionally reverts to description rather than sustained critical evaluation
Limited engagement with counterarguments or alternative explanations of linguistic change

feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs work exhibits consistently relevant research, good analytical depth, and emerging evaluative components, placing it in the upper half of the 4â6 band. While some conclusions rest on descriptive observations and the sample size is limited, the essayâs structured critical insights and coherent argument justify a score of 6, reflecting strong but not exhaustive critical thinking.

Inclusion of all required elements (title page, table of contents, bibliography)
weaknesses: Table of contents page numbers do not align precisely with body pagination
Citation formatting is inconsistent and does not adhere strictly to a single recognised style
Minor typographic issues (e.g., apostrophe omissions, visible formatting tags) detract from professionalism
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets all major organizational requirements and employs a clear structure that supports reader navigation, fitting the 3â4 band. While formatting inconsistencies and citation errors limit a perfect score, the overall layout and formatting considerations are correctly applied, warranting a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student outlines a qualitative methodology centred on semantic and pragmatic analysis of selected tweets, supported by secondary sources on internet linguistics. **Key elementsâdata set, analytical lenses, acknowledgement of biasâare described, showing informed though not exhaustive planning.**  
However, the corpus is limited to a handful of high-profile tweets, and selection criteria (why these tweets, how they represent a decade, sampling procedures) are only briefly mentioned. There is little discussion of alternative methods (e.g., corpus linguistics, frequency data) that would strengthen reliability. Hence the methodology is **mostly complete and relevant but not fully comprehensive or demonstrably rigorous**, aligning with the 3â4 band at the upper end.

Basic layout conventions are met: a title page, table of contents, section headings, in-text citations, and a bibliography are present. However, **formatting inconsistencies are frequent**: the table of contents pagination does not match the body, page numbers are not displayed in the excerpt, and citation formatting deviates from a single recognised style. Minor typographic issues (e.g., inconsistent apostrophes, italic tags showing) detract from professional presentation.  
Given that layout requirements are only partially and inconsistently applied, the strand fits the 1â2 band; the higher mark of 2 recognises that most key elements exist even though they are not fully correct.

The student assembles a **reasonable variety of relevant sources**: academic papers on internet linguistics, dictionary entries, news articles, and primary tweet data. These materials support the exploration of each lexical item and are generally pertinent to the research question.  
Yet, the research remains selective and somewhat anecdotal; only four exemplar tweets form the empirical core, and there is limited engagement with large-scale linguistic data or peer-reviewed studies on Twitter corpora. Consequently, the research is **mostly relevant and sufficient but not exhaustive or consistently high-level**, earning a mid-range score within the 4â6 band.

The studentâs topicâTwitterâs role in changing and popularizing English termsâis introduced in the title, research question and opening paragraphs with **clear purpose and consistent focus**. Throughout the essay the reader is never in doubt about what linguistic phenomenon is being investigated, and each analytical section explicitly links back to the central theme of Twitter-driven lexical change.  
Minor issues of expression (e.g., punctuation and possessive errors such as âTwitters impactâ) occasionally blur precision, and the scope could have been narrowed further than four case studies, but overall the topic is **communicated accurately, explicitly and coherently**, satisfying the descriptors for the 5â6 band. A full 6 is not awarded because the clarity occasionally falters and some digressions (e.g., extended historical overview) weaken the sustained focus.

The discussion is logically organised around four case studies, leading to a coherent if somewhat predictable conclusion that Twitter accelerates lexical change. **Some evaluative comments** (e.g., acknowledgment of methodological bias, reference to critics like Humphrys) demonstrate emerging critical evaluation.  
Yet, the argumentation occasionally becomes descriptive, with limited weighing of alternative explanations (e.g., other social media, broader sociolinguistic forces). Reflections on limitations are brief and not fully integrated into the main argument. Consequently, the work shows **good but not consistently critical discussion**, meriting a score of 4 within the 4â6 band.

The analysis moves beyond description by applying semantic and pragmatic frameworks to each tweet, identifying denotation/connotation and contextual factors. **Connections between tweet content and broader linguistic change are drawn, and some synthesis appears in the conclusion.**  
However, the depth is uneven: explanations often restate dictionary definitions rather than interrogate them, and claims about Twitterâs causal influence are asserted more than demonstrated with empirical evidence. Counter-arguments are acknowledged only briefly. Thus the analysis is **adequate with partial support and some insight but lacks sustained critical depth**, aligning with the lower half of the 4â6 band (score 4).

The essay follows the expected EE structureâtitle page with word count, abstract omitted (as per current EE guidelines), table of contents, introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion, bibliographyâindicating **generally appropriate organisation**. Sections flow in a logical sequence, and internal subheadings aid navigation.  
Nonetheless, the body shows some imbalance (extensive background vs. brief methodology), and the analytical sections occasionally read like discrete mini-essays rather than a fully integrated argument. These minor structural weaknesses place the work comfortably in the 3â4 band but short of flawless execution, so a score of 3 is appropriate.

The essay exhibits **sound knowledge of historical language change, Twitterâs affordances, and specific lexical items**; key concepts such as neologism, semantic shift, and globalization are referenced with supporting academic and journalistic sources. The bibliography is reasonably diverse, combining dictionaries, scholarly articles and reputable media.  
Nevertheless, source integration is sometimes descriptive, with limited critical appraisal of authority or contrasting viewpoints, and a few citations (e.g., blogs, popular press) are relied upon where academic studies could have provided stronger support. Thus the work reflects **good but not exemplary mastery** of topic knowledge and source use, fitting the 3â4 band at a score of 4.

Throughout the essay the student employs linguistic terminologyâsemantic analysis, pragmatics, denotation, connotation, neologism, lexical innovationâwith **mostly accurate meaning and appropriate application**. The terms are integrated naturally into the discussion, signalling a competent command of disciplinary language.  
Minor lapses occur (e.g., conflating âsemanticâ with dictionary definition only; occasional imprecise wording), and explanations sometimes remain surface-level. Overall, however, terminology is **used correctly and consistently enough** to satisfy the 3â4 band at the top end.

The research questionââHow has Twitter changed and popularized English terms over the past decade?ââis **explicitly stated, specific and consistently referenced** during the essayâs development. Each analytical subsection (tweet, hashtag, trolling, cancelled) is overtly tied to this question, demonstrating an effective through-line of inquiry.  
Some breadth issues remain (four examples may not capture the full decade-long evolution, and the question might have been refined to a particular linguistic category or community), but overall the question is **focused and well-integrated** into the discussion, meriting a solid score in the 5â6 band rather than the very highest level.

annotations.comment: The methodology outlines semantic and pragmatic analysis but lacks explicit selection criteria for tweet sampling; specify how tweets were chosen to enhance methodological rigor.
The reading of the wide-eyes emojiâs connotation is insightful and illustrates effective semanticâpragmatic interplay; well-supported within this context.
Citing Goettel (2024) and Metz (2024) for definitions is helpful, but incorporating peer-reviewed theoretical sources on semantics and pragmatics would strengthen conceptual depth.
The consistent use of subheadings for each term enhances the visual hierarchy and allows for effective sectional division in the analysis.
The comparative definitions of âtrollingâ illustrate semantic shift, but critically evaluating which definition best aligns with Twitter dynamics would strengthen your argument.
The examination of @SamsungMobileUSâs tweet is apt, yet referencing additional brand interactions could provide a more representative sample of trolling behavior on Twitter.
Citing the emoji for connotation analysis is appropriate, yet support this interpretation with a peer-reviewed source on emoji pragmatics to strengthen evidence.
The critique referencing Humphrys (2007) would benefit from engaging counterarguments to avoid a one-sided evaluation and deepen critical analysis.
The reflective acknowledgment of methodological limitations and suggestions for future data sampling demonstrates thoughtful evaluation of the research design.
The proposal to extend research across platforms is promising; define specific comparative methodologies to ensure these extensions remain focused and feasible.
Several sources in this section are not cited in the main text; ensure every bibliography entry directly supports content discussed to maintain coherence between research and references.
The acknowledgment of personal bias is a valuable reflexive move, but reflect analytically on how this bias may specifically influence interpretation of examples.
The use of clear subheadings for each case study (e.g., âTweetâ) improves readability and helps structure the analytical sections effectively.
The semantic discussion of Ellen DeGeneresâs tweet is insightful but relies on a single example; incorporate additional tweets or dataset metrics to substantiate claims of lexical change.
The pragmatic analysis of early hashtag reception demonstrates a strong understanding of context and user attitudes; this depth adds meaningful nuance to the case study.
Labelling the hashtag discussion as its own subsection guides the reader through distinct analytical stages and maintains structural coherence.
The analysis highlights multilingual hashtag usage, yet integrating usage frequency data or comparative corpus examples would bolster claims regarding cross-linguistic impact.
Relying solely on a single source for the transformation of âtrollingâ limits the perspective; consult additional academic literature or specialized studies to enrich the etymology discussion.
The retrieval dates are presented inconsistently; standardize date formats (e.g., YYYY-MM-DD) for all online sources to improve impression of professionalism.
Some listed sources (e.g., generic articles on hashtag addition) are tangential; critically assess each referenceâs relevance to ensure the bibliography remains focused.
URLs with embedded tracking or query parameters clutter citations; use clean, persistent links (e.g., DOI or direct resource URL) for a concise bibliography.
The citation for DeGeneres (2009) differs in format from other entries; ensure all bibliographic entries adhere to the selected citation style guidelines consistently.
The research question is explicitly stated and consistently tied to the investigation, providing a strong focus and through-line for the essay.
The title contains a punctuation omission: use an apostrophe in âTwitterâs impact on Englishâ to maintain accuracy and clarity.
The inclusion of the word count at the start of the document meets the formatting requirement effectively.
The clearly labeled âTable of Contentsâ heading aids navigation and reflects appropriate organizational structure.
The page numbers in the table of contents do not align precisely with the body pagination; verify and update to ensure accurate navigation.
The colloquial example effectively contrasts academic and informal registers, but the transition into this illustration interrupts scholarly tone; consider reframing to maintain academic rigor.
The extensive historical overview of English evolution, while informative, diverges from the core focus on Twitter; streamline this section to reinforce the research question.
The use of (Ellis, 2022; Loach, n.d.) provides background, but integrating critical evaluation of these sources would strengthen the foundation of your argument.
The background section effectively describes internet-driven change, but it remains largely descriptive; incorporate critical analysis of the identified studies to deepen understanding.
The discussion of social mediaâs global reach is pertinent, yet the scope could be narrowed further to focus specifically on Twitterâs unique affordances.
The transition to the âCancelledâ case study is abrupt; consider summarizing previous findings in a brief connective sentence to maintain narrative flow.
The historical narrative on cancel culture is thorough but dominates this section; balance storytelling with analytical commentary to maintain academic focus.
The conclusion succinctly recaps methodology and findings, reinforcing the investigationâs focus and demonstrating coherent synthesis.
The âBibliographyâ heading is clear, but ensure it follows the same formatting (font, size, spacing) as other section headers for consistency.
The bibliography entries mix italics and plain text unpredictably; adopt a single recognized citation style and apply it uniformly across all entries.
